sections.summary: Trimethoprim, most often given as trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, can precipitate severe, sometimes fatal bone marrow suppression when combined with methotrexate.
sections.mechanism: Both drugs inhibit dihydrofolate reductase, so their antifolate effects are additive rather than merely overlapping. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole also reduces renal tubular secretion of methotrexate and displaces it from plasma proteins, raising free methotrexate concentrations.
sections.clinical_effects: Pancytopenia, severe mucositis, and megaloblastic anemia can develop, occasionally within days, particularly in patients on higher-dose or even low-dose weekly methotrexate regimens.
sections.management: Avoid trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in patients taking methotrexate whenever possible; select an alternative antibiotic. If no alternative exists, use it only briefly with close hematologic monitoring and consider folinic acid rescue.
sections.monitoring: Obtain a complete blood count before and shortly after starting the combination, and watch closely for mouth sores, fever, or unusual bruising that could signal marrow suppression.

evidence.citation: Bactrim (sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim) FDA prescribing information, Drug Interactions section
